L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched in conditions that were explicitly identified as terrible by his companions, with sustained strong gusts and horizontal flags at the restaurant (indicating winds well above any safe threshold for BASE). Two companions declined to jump and directly warned him. The wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration capability, pushing him back into the wall. The decision to jump in these conditions was the producing mechanism for the fatality — execution was adequate (good exit, on-heading opening, full flight attempting to penetrate), but no amount of skill could overcome wind that exceeded the canopy's airspeed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouted during packing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper routed the bridle around the right leg strap during packing, preventing the pilot chute from extracting the canopy. This rigging error directly produced a total malfunction (PC in tow with container closed), which was the mechanism of death. The jumper intended correct rigging but failed in execution, likely due to rushing through the packing process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying proximity in flat light conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line on a day with heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, and no texture on the snowfield — complete flat light conditions. These conditions are known to eliminate depth cues essential for terrain flying. The decision to fly proximity to terrain in conditions that made altitude assessment impossible was the producing mechanism: he could not perceive his altitude and flew into the ground at full speed with no deployment attempt. This was not an execution error — his flight technique was textbook — but the decision to fly near terrain when visual depth perception was fundamentally compromised was clearly and specifically inadequate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly unsafe wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts well over 30 knots from the side, conditions that four experienced companions unanimously deemed unsafe and stood down from. The domain reference states few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and no objects are good over 20 mph; these conditions (35+ mph gusts) were far beyond any acceptable threshold. The crosswind gust caught him immediately on exit, pushing him sideways and back into the wall, directly producing the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper spent the entire freefall attempting to recover stability rather than deploying the pilot chute. Despite approximately 6 seconds of freefall from a slider-up cliff, he fixated on regaining a belly-to-earth position and never extracted the PC from the BOC, impacting the talus with the system still closed. The domain reference is explicit: whatever happens, pull — deploying low on your back with an off-heading is bad, but beats impact with nothing out. Priority order is (1) deploy, (2) deploy at the right altitude, (3) deploy in stable body position.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with poor push-off
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's legs buckled on the push-off, producing an immediately unstable exit that rolled him onto his back into a slow tumble. This poor exit technique eliminated the separation and stable body position needed for a controlled deployment sequence, and consumed the limited freefall time with recovery attempts rather than deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped unfamiliar suit at demanding site
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump a tracking suit from an intermediate-to-advanced cliff requiring mandatory tracking past a technical section where the wall comes back in, despite having only two skydives in the suit and zero cliff jumps with it. The domain reference establishes that a tracking suit requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and the jumper must have found the ideal body position for maximum forward speed and glide before jumping it from a cliff. With only 2 skydives in the suit, the jumper lacked the foundational ability to fly it effectively, making the decision to use it at this site the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome — he could not generate the forward separation required to clear the technical section regardless of execution quality on the day.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incorrect off-heading correction technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 180° off-heading opening facing the building, the jumper grabbed only the right toggle and pulled it hard. This single-brake input created a diving turn into the building rather than arresting forward speed. Accepted practice for a 180° off-heading is to first apply both rear risers (or both brakes) to halt forward movement toward the object, then release one side to turn away. The asymmetric single-toggle input was a technique execution error that directly produced the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in zero visibility
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit in conditions where visibility was essentially zero — the fjord, landing area, and wall were not visible. This decision was clearly and specifically wrong: jumping a terminal cliff with no ability to see terrain features, including a known protruding ledge 120 meters below, made it impossible to navigate freefall safely. He tracked into an unseen ledge and impacted in terminal freefall with his pilot chute still stowed, meaning the zero-visibility decision directly produced the fatal outcome regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure during tracking
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Marcus reached up to adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during the tracking phase, which caused him to abandon his tracking body position and go flat. This loss of active tracking resulted in leftward drift toward the wall. He failed to monitor his proximity to terrain and made no corrective input despite the ledge being in clear view for several seconds, ultimately impacting in terminal freefall without deploying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued proximity flying despite known skill deficiency
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper explicitly recognized that his angle of attack control was inadequate, had experienced at least four near-miss stalls in the preceding three months, and identified the specific corrective action needed (returning to skydiving for stall recovery training). Despite this clear self-assessment, he consciously chose to continue flying wingsuit BASE at cliffs every weekend without addressing the deficiency. This was not an execution error on the fatal jump—the decision to keep exposing himself to proximity terrain flying with a known, repeated, and acknowledged inability to maintain adequate airspeed was the producing mechanism. The stall that killed him was the same failure mode he had identified and failed to correct.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to perform heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading pointing directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away. Despite having approximately two seconds and both riser and toggle inputs available, the jumper made no corrective input whatsoever — his arms remained at his sides. Multiple witnesses confirmed he took no action. This failure to execute any heading correction, a fundamental and practiced skill for antenna jumps, was the direct mechanism of the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with known-damaged PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was aware of a significant tear (~4 inches) in his pilot chute mesh, was advised by companions to replace it, was offered spare PCs, yet consciously decided the damaged PC would still function adequately. This decision was clearly and specifically wrong: a PC with a large tear in the mesh cannot reliably generate sufficient drag to extract the packjob, and the jumper had both the knowledge and the means to use a functional PC. The damaged PC failed to inflate, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow) and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to check airspace before deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David deployed his pilot chute directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy after tracking over that jumper. The narrative explicitly states he did not check his airspace before deploying. This is an attention/awareness failure during execution — he failed to monitor his position relative to other jumpers before initiating deployment, which is a fundamental execution requirement in multi-way jumps. The deployment into another jumper's canopy caused the fatal entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Deliberately excessive delay
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a clean exit, good track, and no equipment or weather issues. He consciously chose to extend his delay beyond what the altitude could support, a pattern of progressively longer delays (4 to 6 to 8+ seconds) that other jumpers had warned him about repeatedly. On this jump he deliberately pulled too low for the canopy to complete extraction before impact. The decision to pull at that altitude was the producing mechanism for the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of airspeed control leading to stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Midway through the flight, the jumper's angle of attack shifted and he stalled the wingsuit. His glide transitioned from shallow to nearly vertical over approximately two seconds, and he sank out rapidly without recovering. This is a performance/execution failure — he was flying an appropriate line but failed to maintain adequate airspeed and angle of attack, entering an unrecoverable stall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted jump far beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives, zero BASE jumps, and zero wingsuit skydives, yet attempted a slider-up terminal cliff jump wearing a tracking suit. Accepted progression norms require a minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, and tracking suit BASE jumping requires prior mastery of both regular BASE jumping and tracking suit skydiving. This decision represents a massive departure from what any competent jumper would consider appropriate, and the complete absence of foundational skills directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and inadequate separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an unstable exit resulting in insufficient forward separation from the cliff. With zero BASE experience and minimal skydiving experience, the jumper lacked the fundamental body position skills, sub-terminal tracking ability, and deployment skills needed to maintain object separation. The jumper impacted the terrain near the exit point, with the canopy partially deployed, indicating failure to achieve adequate separation through tracking and/or failure to deploy at a survivable altitude in a stable position.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Packing execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container underneath the closing flaps instead of routing it out to the BOC pouch. When he reached for the BOC handle on deployment, the handle extracted cleanly but the PC remained trapped inside the closed container, resulting in a total malfunction. This was a fundamental execution error in a known procedural step of the pack job.
